content: Hey Saturday, I'm trying to plan out my weekend and I'm a bit overwhelmed with options. My partner and I want to go out to eat Saturday night, but we're also thinking about hitting up the farmers market Sunday morning to get fresh ingredients. What do you think would be a good approach to balance both?

That sounds like a great weekend! Here's how I'd think about it: if you're planning to grab fresh ingredients at the farmers market on Sunday, you could use those for meal prep and lunches throughout the week. So for Saturday dinner, I'd suggest picking a restaurant that's different from what you'd normally cook at home—maybe something with a cuisine you don't make often, or a place known for technique-heavy dishes. That way you get to enjoy eating out while still maintaining that farm-to-table momentum Sunday. What kind of cuisines are you two usually drawn to? That'll help narrow down restaurant options.

We usually cook Italian at home pretty frequently, so we're trying to move away from that. We're both really into Asian food—Thai, Japanese, Korean—but we also love a good farm-to-table restaurant. There's this new place called Ember & Root that opened up a couple months ago that's supposed to be amazing for seasonal ingredients. Have you heard anything about it?

Ember & Root sounds perfect for what you're describing! I don't have real-time reviews, but a farm-to-table spot with seasonal focus would actually pair beautifully with your farmers market plans. You'd see what's in season at the market Sunday, and then you'd already have experienced how a professional kitchen showcases those same ingredients Saturday night—great learning opportunity if you like cooking. For the Asian restaurants though, does your area have any newer Thai or Korean spots that focus on fresh ingredients? Sometimes those cuisines align really well with farmers market produce—think Thai basil, scallions, mushrooms, fresh chilies. What neighborhoods are you willing to travel to, and what's your budget looking like for Saturday dinner?

We're in the downtown area but happy to drive about 20 minutes in any direction. Budget-wise, we're thinking $80-120 per person including drinks, so we want something nice but not crazy high-end. I actually found this Korean place called Hanaro that gets really good press for doing seasonal small plates. Would that be a good fit?

Hanaro sounds excellent for your criteria—Korean small plates are inherently designed around fresh, seasonal ingredients, and that price point usually means you get really solid quality without the stuffy atmosphere. Small plates are also perfect for trying multiple things and experiencing different flavor profiles in one meal. The fact that they do seasonal preparations means they're actively thinking about what's available, which connects nicely to your Sunday farmers market trip. One thing I'd suggest: when you make your reservation, if they have it available online, check out their current menu to see what seasonal items are featured. That way you can already start thinking about what those ingredients might be and what the market will likely have. Are you planning to do any meal prep Sunday, or is the farmers market trip more for the week's cooking?

Yeah, we usually try to do some light meal prep on Sunday—nothing too intense, but like prepping proteins and cutting veggies so weeknight cooking is easier. We're thinking of going to the farmers market around 9am before it gets too crowded. What should we prioritize buying there given that we're planning to cook during the week?

Nine AM is smart timing—you'll beat the crowds and have first pick of stuff. For a good meal prep foundation, I'd focus on: versatile proteins (if they have them, pastured chicken or ground beef), hearty vegetables that keep well (carrots, beets, squash, Brussels sprouts), fresh greens for salads, and herbs (cilantro, parsley, basil last longer than you'd think if you treat them right). Mid-October farmers markets usually have great root vegetables, greens, and maybe some late-season berries. Since you cook Asian food regularly, definitely grab Thai basil, fresh ginger, garlic, and scallions if they have them—those prep beautifully and store well in the fridge. One pro tip: bring cash and a list, but leave room for spontaneity. Often the best meal ideas come from whatever looks freshest. Are you planning specific meals for the week, or more of a 'buy good stuff and figure it out' approach?

We do the 'buy good stuff and figure it out' approach mostly, but my partner has been wanting to try making Korean-style braised vegetables, so maybe we could focus on things that work for that. Also, I'm a bit worried about produce going bad—we've had issues before where stuff spoils midweek. How long do different vegetables actually last?

Great question—this is where farmers market timing gets tricky because produce is fresher but sometimes ripens faster. Here's a rough guide: hearty root vegetables (carrots, beets, radishes) last 2-3 weeks in the crisper drawer, which is why they're meal-prep gold. Leafy greens (kale, spinach, bok choy) last about 5-7 days if stored properly in a container with paper towels to absorb moisture. Mushrooms last about a week, and more delicate stuff like cilantro or Thai basil maybe 5-10 days if kept in a jar with water like flowers. For Korean braised vegetables, you'd actually want sturdy stuff—daikon radish, napa cabbage, carrots, and maybe some gobo root if they have it. These are perfect because they braise beautifully and last ages. My suggestion: buy the heartier vegetables in bigger quantities, and save the delicate herbs and greens for mid-week. Does your farmers market have specific vendors you've shopped with before, or will this be a first visit?

It's been a couple years since we've been to this particular market, so it's basically starting fresh. I'm worried we'll show up and not know what to buy or where to start. Should we map out specific vendors beforehand, or just wander and see what looks good?

Honestly, I'd suggest a hybrid approach. Most farmers markets have vendor directories online or posted at the entrance—take 10 minutes Friday to see if there are specific vendors selling things you want (like someone who specializes in Asian vegetables, or a trusted meat vendor). That gives you a loose game plan so you're not completely lost. Then when you get there, absolutely wander and see what catches your eye, ask vendors about what's peak season right now, and strike up conversations—farmers markets are great for that. People selling there usually have great suggestions for preparation. Since you mentioned Korean braised veg specifically, look for vendors with lots of root vegetables and any Asian-specific produce—daikon, bok choy, napa cabbage. One practical thing: bring a list of 5-7 'anchor' ingredients you know you'll use (like eggs, butter, whatever proteins you like), and fill the rest of your bag with seasonal inspiration. That keeps you from wandering too far into 'ooh interesting but what do I do with this?' territory.

That makes sense. So for the anchor ingredients—should I get those at the farmers market or just hit up the grocery store first? Like, I know regular butter is pretty standard stuff, but farmers market butter is supposedly way different. Is it worth the premium?

Good instinct—farmers market butter is genuinely different because it's usually made from pastured cream, which has higher fat content and more complex flavor. It's noticeably better for finishing dishes, making pastry, or eating straight on toast. That said, for cooking anchor ingredients like eggs and butter, I'd actually suggest: buy your shelf-stable basics (flour, oil, salt, spices) at the grocery store, but get eggs and butter from the farmers market if they have them. The quality difference on eggs is huge—real free-range farmers market eggs have richer yolks and taste better. For butter, one good stick from the farmers market goes a long way and lasts weeks, so it's worth it. Skip the grocery store butter that week. As for proteins though, I'd still buy meat or fish at a specialty butcher or grocery store unless you've vetted a specific vendors at the market. Does that make sense? What proteins are you and your partner eating most regularly?

We eat a lot of chicken, some beef for weekend cooking, and we try to get fish once a week or so. I've been trying to get more comfortable with cooking whole fish since it's more sustainable, but honestly I'm kind of intimidated. Do you think the farmers market is a good place to buy whole fish for a beginner?

Actually, yes—farmers markets can be perfect for this because the fishmongers are usually super approachable and want to help. They can show you how to check for freshness (clear eyes, firm flesh, bright gills), and here's the key thing: if you buy a whole fish from them, ask them to clean it, descale it, or even gut it for you. Many vendors will do this on the spot, which removes a huge barrier to entry. Plus, because the fish is fresh and the vendor actually knows their product, you'll have a better experience than with prepackaged grocery store fish. Start with something forgiving like branzino or small snapper rather than a delicate white fish. I'd suggest buying one whole fish this weekend, asking the vendor for a quick prep tip, and trying it Sunday or Monday when you're not too tired. Your partner could even make it a little project together. Have you cooked much fish at all, or is whole fish the thing that's intimidating you?

I've done fillets plenty of times, but whole fish just feels different—like all those eyes staring at you and the bones and everything. But I like the sustainability angle and the flavor is apparently way better. Maybe if I start with a smaller whole fish it'll feel less intense? My partner has done whole fish before but hasn't in years. We could team up on it.

Absolutely—teaming up on it is perfect and honestly makes it fun instead of intimidating. Start with something small and manageable, maybe 12-14 inches long, so it feels less daunting. The eyes thing is funny but totally gets easier the first time you realize they're just, you know, fish eyes and not judgmental. Once you've done one whole fish successfully, the mystique completely disappears and you'll wonder why you were ever nervous. For prep, a really simple approach is: get it cleaned at the market, pat it dry when you get home, season generously inside and out with salt and whatever herbs are handy (those farmers market herbs!), maybe a squeeze of lemon, and either pan-sear it or roast it. Takes like 15-20 minutes total. Since you're doing meal prep Sunday, maybe make this your Monday or Tuesday dinner so you're not tired, and you can give it full attention. Once you nail your first one, you'll be buying whole fish all the time. Should we circle back to the Saturday dinner reservation—do you want help thinking through what to order at Hanaro?

Yeah, good idea. So with small plates at a Korean restaurant, what's the strategy for ordering? Like, how many plates should we get, and are there certain categories we should make sure to hit? I don't want to order too little and be hungry, but also don't want to waste food.

Smart thinking—small plates are all about balance and strategy. Here's what I'd do: for two people, aim for 5-7 plates total, which usually lands you around $60-80 before drinks (well within your budget). The strategy is to hit different categories: one or two with protein (beef, seafood, pork—something with substance), one or two vegetable dishes (since you're already thinking about vegetables for Sunday!), something fermented or pickle-based (kimchi or banchan-style), and maybe one lighter thing like a seafood or dumpling situation. Definitely ask your server about seasonal specialties—that's where the good stuff is. Also, pro tip: order about half your dishes at once, and then order the second round after eating those, so everything's fresh and you're not overwhelmed with plates at once. This also gives you time to talk and enjoy it rather than cramming. Korean small plates are designed to be eaten as you go anyway. Would you want to go in with a loose plan of what you're looking for, or prefer to just follow the server's recommendations?

I think following the server's recommendations is the way to go, especially since they'll know what's seasonal. But I'm wondering about one thing—should we avoid ordering anything we could easily make at home? Like, if we're cooking Asian food all the time anyway, should we treat this as a chance to eat things we wouldn't typically try?

That's actually really smart thinking—since you cook Asian regularly and are going to the farmers market tomorrow, this is your chance to order things that are either hard to replicate at home or showcase restaurant technique. Skip the simple vegetable side dishes you could make anytime. Instead, lean into things that require specialized ingredients, equipment, or technique: whole grilled fish, something with complex braising, marinated raw preparations, anything with house-made fermented components, or dishes with specialty cuts of meat. These are things where the restaurant's skill and sourcing really matter. That said, don't skip a vegetable dish entirely—but choose one that's clearly the showcase rather than the side. Maybe ask your server, 'What's the most technically impressive vegetable dish you have right now?' instead of just 'what vegetables do you have?' That frames it as you're interested in artistry, not just eating vegetables. This approach also means you'll learn something by eating there, which is fun. Does your partner tend to be more adventurous with food, or do you two have different approaches?

We're both pretty adventurous, honestly. My partner will eat basically anything, but they're kind of a perfectionist about flavors—like they notice when something's slightly off-balance. I'm more of an 'try whatever' person but I get annoyed if something tastes boring. So between us we're pretty opinionated about food, which is why the restaurant choice matters so much. Does that change how you'd approach the ordering strategy?

Oh, that actually helps a lot—and honestly, that's the best kind of couple to go to a good restaurant with because you'll actually appreciate the effort. Your partner's palate-sensitivity means they'll catch nuance and technique, and your openness means you'll try things they might skip. With that in mind, I'd tell your server upfront: 'We're excited about seasonal flavors and technique—what are you really proud of right now?' This signals that you're not just hungry, you actually care about the food. Then you can absolutely ask adventurous questions like 'what's the most interesting thing you've been cooking with lately?' or 'what's the hardest dish to execute properly on your menu?' Servers at good restaurants love when people engage like this, and they'll direct you toward things worth ordering. Also, since your partner notices balance, if a dish tastes slightly off or underflavored to them, that's actually useful feedback to share (kindly) with the restaurant—good spots appreciate it. But I'm guessing at a new place like Hanaro with good press, everything will be pretty dialed in. When are you planning to make the reservation?

We were thinking of going Saturday around 7pm, so I should probably call or book online today or tomorrow to secure a spot. Is 7pm a good time to go, or would you recommend earlier or later? I'm also wondering if we should mention anything special when booking—like, is it worth noting that we're celebrating something, or should we just make a regular reservation?

Seven PM is actually a sweet spot—it's early enough that you won't feel rushed, but late enough that the kitchen's warm and running smoothly (they're past the initial dinner rush). If you can do 7:15 or 7:30, even better, but 7pm is totally fine. As for mentioning something special: only do that if it's actually true (anniversary, birthday, promotion, etc.). Restaurants prefer to make a good meal special rather than feel like they're being nudged into making something special artificially. That said, if it's just a 'we're celebrating choosing to go out together after a busy week' situation, which is honestly nice enough, you don't need to manufacture a narrative. What you *should* mention when booking: let them know your party size (2 people), and if they have a field for dietary restrictions or preferences, you could put something like 'adventurous eaters, interested in seasonal specialties'—that's actually helpful info for the restaurant. Call or book online today if you can—Saturday nights fill up, especially at new popular places. Do you have their number or website handy?

I'll look up the number and website today. Quick question though—should we eat beforehand or go in hungry? I've heard some conflicting advice on this. Like, sometimes people say never eat before going out to a nice restaurant, but other times people say a light snack helps you enjoy it more. What's actually best?

Great question and honestly, it depends on the individual, but here's my take: go in genuinely hungry but not ravenous. A light snack 2-3 hours before (like a small salad or piece of fruit around 4pm) means you won't be uncomfortable or too full, but you'll still have real appetite when you sit down. That sweet spot means you'll taste and enjoy each plate more, and you'll be able to pace yourself naturally rather than either scarfing everything down or feeling too full halfway through. The 'never eat beforehand' advice is a bit much—you want to be able to concentrate and taste, which is hard when your stomach's growling. The 'eat a full meal beforehand' approach defeats the point of going to a restaurant. So light snack in early afternoon, maybe a walk around the neighborhood, and you'll be in perfect headspace. Also, drinking water throughout the day helps a lot. After eating at Hanaro, you'll probably feel good and maybe want to take a walk or grab a drink somewhere else, which is nice. Are you two planning to do anything else Saturday besides dinner, or keeping the day pretty open?

We're keeping Saturday pretty chill actually—maybe a farmers market trip in the morning to browse and plan Sunday's buying, or a walk somewhere nice. We want to save energy for dinner and enjoy it without feeling rushed. Then Sunday is obviously the market trip and meal prep. I'm just trying to figure out the timing of everything so we're not exhausted.

That's a really smart approach—you're planning your energy, which honestly shows mature weekend planning. Here's a timeline that would work well: Saturday morning, do a casual walk or farmers market browse (light activity, no heavy lifting). Grab lunch around noon—something filling but not too heavy—then have some downtime in early afternoon (nap, read, whatever). Light snack around 4pm, get ready without rushing, head to dinner at 7pm. You'll feel great and present. Sunday, get to the farmers market around 9am (sounds like you already planned this), which is when you'll actually do the real shopping and buying. That gives you Sunday afternoon for meal prep—maybe 2-3 hours of chopping, organizing, and prepping proteins. This spreads the activity across both days without cramming everything in. One thing though: if Saturday morning is a farmers market browse, don't buy stuff then—just look, take notes on what looked good, and buy Sunday. Otherwise you're dealing with produce deteriorating Saturday night. Sound like a good rhythm?

Perfect, that makes a lot of sense. So for the Sunday farmers market, we're bringing bags and cash, going early, with a loose list of anchor items but leaving room for seasonal stuff. We'll focus on things that work for Korean braised vegetables plus some good proteins. One thing I didn't think about—how early is too early to get there, and when does it usually get too crowded to enjoy it?

Most farmers markets open at 9am (some at 8am), so arriving right at opening or 15 minutes after is ideal. You'll beat 90% of the crowds and vendors are fresh and not picked-over. By 10:30 or 11am, it gets noticeably busier. By noon, it's pretty packed and some vendors are running low on popular items. So your 9am plan is actually perfect—you'll have the market mostly to yourself, everything's fully stocked, and you're back home by 10:30am with plenty of afternoon for meal prep. One logistical tip: bring more bags than you think you need (or a small cooler if you're buying fish or meat), wear comfortable shoes, and bring your phone for photos if you want to remember vendor names or locations. Also, maybe set aside like $100 in cash (farmers markets aren't always card-friendly everywhere), and bring a list on your phone rather than paper so you can edit as you go. Since you're doing light meal prep Sunday afternoon, getting home by 10:30 gives you the whole day. Are you planning to do your prepping together, or split tasks?

We usually do it together—one person preps proteins while the other handles vegetables, then we rotate. We've gotten pretty efficient at it, maybe 2.5 hours total for a week's worth of stuff. The thing I'm wondering about is storage—we have decent containers but I'm not sure if we're organizing things right. How should we actually store prepped ingredients so they last the whole week?

Good system you've got. For storage, the main principle is: keep raw proteins separate from vegetables, and group by how soon you'll use things. Here's the breakdown: cooked proteins (if you're precooking them) go in airtight containers on the middle shelf where it's coldest. Raw or prepped vegetables go in containers or bags with a paper towel to absorb moisture—this prevents them from getting slimy. Delicate herbs and greens go in separate containers. Keep your most-used items (like prepped chicken or that bag of green beans you'll use Wednesday) at eye level on your fridge so you actually see them and use them. Label everything with date and contents if you're organized like that—it helps you remember what you made and when. Raw vegetables last longer than cooked ones, so if you're prepping for 7 days, prep sturdy raw vegetables (carrots, beets, root veg) and cook more delicate stuff midweek. For the braised vegetables your partner wants to make, you could actually do a batch of those Sunday and portion them—they keep 5 days easy. Does your fridge have a crisper drawer? That changes the strategy slightly.

Yeah, we have crisper drawers. So should we be using those for everything, or are there things that shouldn't go in them? I've been kind of just throwing stuff in there haphazardly and I bet that's why some stuff goes bad.

That's probably part of it, yeah. Crisper drawers are amazing but they need some strategy. Here's the thing: crispers work by adjusting humidity. One drawer is typically set to high humidity (for things that wilt, like greens and herbs), and one to low humidity (for things that rot, like tomatoes, peppers, apples). Leafy greens, herbs, and anything delicate goes in the high-humidity drawer. Sturdy vegetables like carrots, beets, squash, broccoli, cabbage go in the low-humidity drawer or even just on regular shelves. Here's the key mistake people make: they throw everything in together and don't organize it. What works well is: keep things you'll use first toward the front and on top, so you actually see and use them before they go bad. Rotate older stuff forward when you buy new stuff. Also, don't wash delicate greens until you're using them—wash and dry just what you need. The paper towel trick I mentioned works great for containers of greens too. Since you're getting fresh farmers market stuff Sunday, you'll probably see a huge difference in how long things last because it's fresher than grocery store stuff. Try organizing your crispers this way after your farmers market trip and see if it helps.

This is really helpful. I feel way more organized about the weekend now. One last thing—since we're buying all this seasonal produce, are there any preservation techniques we should learn? Like, is it worth learning to pickle things or ferment stuff, or is that overkill for a home cook?

Not overkill at all, actually—it's easier than you think and makes using up farmers market stuff way more manageable. Quick pickling is genuinely easy: just vinegar, salt, sugar, and hot water poured over vegetables, and boom—they last weeks. You could do quick-pickled radishes, cucumbers, or onions in like 15 minutes while you're doing meal prep. Fermentation (like simple lacto-fermentation for kraut or kimchi) is a bit more involved but honestly not complicated, just takes time. That said, I wouldn't jump into learning both this weekend. Maybe focus on getting your regular meal prep system dialed in first, and then next month if you're sitting on extra farmers market pickles or cabbages, try one quick pickle recipe. Korean fermented stuff actually pairs perfectly with your cooking interests though—you could make a simple kimchi or pick some up at the market. Here's my suggestion: this Sunday, focus on prepping and storing things correctly. Once you get through a full week eating what you prepped, you'll understand what actually gets used and what goes bad, and that informs future projects. Does that feel manageable?

Yeah, that makes sense. I don't want to overcommit and then feel overwhelmed. Alright, I think I have a solid plan now. Saturday: chill morning, light lunch, dinner at Hanaro at 7pm. Sunday: farmers market at 9am, back by 10:30, meal prep for the afternoon. One thing though—should we make a backup plan in case Hanaro is fully booked? Like, what other restaurants should I have in mind just in case?

Good contingency thinking. Since you were interested in Hanaro for the seasonal Korean small plates angle, I'd keep a backup that hits a similar vibe rather than just any restaurant. Do you have any Thai or Japanese spots in your area known for seasonal cooking? Or if you want to go full backup mode, maybe that Ember & Root you mentioned earlier could work for Saturday instead of Sunday's farmers market prep (though it'd probably be more expensive and require more energy). Another option: if Hanaro doesn't have a table, ask if they can seat you at the bar for a more casual vibe—sometimes that's still available. Or check their availability for 8:15pm or 9:30pm instead of 7pm. I'd call them first thing today or tomorrow and book your first choice, and only pivot if they say they're fully booked. But having 1-2 backup options in mind (written down, prices noted) means you won't panic if it doesn't work out. What's another restaurant in your area that fits that 'seasonal, quality-focused' style you two seem to love?

There's a Japanese place called Mizuki that does an omakase-style menu, which could be cool. But I'm worried omakase might be too formal or stuffy, and also I don't know how much it costs. For our vibe this weekend, I feel like the small plates at Hanaro still feels more right. Should I just be confident in Hanaro and not overthink the backup?

Honestly, yes—commit to Hanaro. It checks all your boxes: it's new and exciting, seasonal small plates are perfect for two people, the price point works, it matches what you two are interested in (fresh ingredients, technique, flavor balance), and it feeds into your farmers market plans. Omakase can be amazing but it's a different energy (more formal, sitting at a bar, less interactive ordering)—it's not the same as what you're looking for this weekend. Unless Hanaro straight-up tells you they're booked for the next month (unlikely), book it and go. That kind of commitment actually makes for better nights out—you're excited about the specific choice rather than second-guessing. Call them today or tomorrow and you'll probably get your 7pm spot. If you can't, ask about 7:30pm or 8pm instead. But don't overthink it. You've already done the research, you have good reasons for choosing it, and it fits your weekend perfectly. Go with that confidence.

You're right. I'm going to call them today and book it. One final question—what should I wear? Is there a dress code at these kinds of seasonal small plate restaurants? I don't want to be overdressed or underdressed.

Most contemporary seasonal small plate restaurants are pretty casual-smart these days. I'd aim for what I call 'elevated casual'—nice jeans or chinos, a clean shirt or sweater, maybe a blazer if you have one and feel like it. Definitely not t-shirt and sweatpants casual, but also not suit-and-tie formal. Think 'I made an effort but I'm also comfortable.' Since Hanaro is newer and positioned as approachable (not fine dining stuffy), you can safely go on the more casual side of that spectrum. Check their website or Instagram if they have photos of the space and other diners—that often gives you a vibe check. But honestly, most people at good restaurants that aren't ultra-formal are in exactly this kind of outfit, so you'll blend right in. The fact that you're thinking about it means you'll probably get it right. Wear whatever makes you feel good and confident—that energy matters more than the exact outfit. Does that help?

Yeah, that helps a lot. I think I'm all set now. Just to recap the whole weekend—Saturday is chill morning, lunch, light snack, Hanaro at 7pm. Sunday is farmers market at 9am, home by 10:30, meal prep in the afternoon. We're focusing on seasonal stuff that makes sense for Korean cooking, gonna be thoughtful about storage, and I'm gonna call Hanaro today to book. This actually feels really manageable now instead of overwhelming.

Perfect—you've got a solid plan that balances treating yourself (nice dinner) with being productive and thoughtful about food (farmers market, meal prep). The fact that everything connects—eating at Hanaro Saturday preps your brain for what's seasonal, then Sunday at the market you see those actual ingredients, and then meal prep brings it all together—that's honestly a really nice weekend. You're being intentional without overthinking it, which is the sweet spot. One last thing: enjoy Saturday dinner without any phone distractions if you can. Be present with your partner, taste the food, let the server surprise you. That's where the real value is. Then Sunday when you're at the market and prepping, you'll already have those fresh flavors and techniques in mind. Good luck with the Hanaro reservation, and have an amazing weekend. You've clearly thought this through well.

Thanks so much for walking me through all of this. I really appreciate it. One tiny thing I just realized—should we have any dietary preferences or allergies when we book Hanaro? Like, should I mention anything about how we eat, or just keep it simple?

Keep it simple unless you have actual allergies or strict dietary restrictions (vegetarian, vegan, nut allergy, etc.). If you two eat pretty much everything and aren't avoiding anything, just don't mention anything—let them recommend freely. Restaurants love that because it means they can show off their full range. If one of you avoids something (like mushrooms or shellfish), definitely mention it when booking so the kitchen can adjust. But 'we like to try everything' honestly serves you better at a place like Hanaro because they'll give you their best and most interesting stuff without hesitation. Since you mentioned being adventurous eaters earlier, you're all set to not need any special notes. Just book the table under your name, confirm party of 2, and show up ready to eat. That's it.
Perfect. Alright, I'm definitely calling today. Thanks again for all the help planning this out. I feel way less scattered now and actually excited about the weekend instead of stressed about it.

That's exactly what I hoped for—weekends should feel exciting, not stressful. You've got a really thoughtful plan that includes fun (great dinner), practical productivity (meal prep), and time to enjoy good food with your partner. Those are the best weekends. Go make that Hanaro reservation, enjoy Saturday night, have fun at the farmers market Sunday morning, and feel good about being set up for a solid week of cooking. You've got this. Have an amazing weekend!
